title: Berlin Street Art - Quick Summary
items: **Berlin street art mostly sits outside the polished centre.** The famous walls are a short train ride out, in Friedrichshain, Kreuzberg and Schöneberg, and almost all of it is free to see.

**The easiest spot is Haus Schwarzenberg,** a scruffy courtyard at Rosenthaler Straße 39 by Hackescher Markt, layered with ever-changing paste-ups and murals. It is free, open at any hour, and about five minutes from where my tour ends.

**The famous one is the East Side Gallery,** a 1.3 km painted stretch of the Berlin Wall with the Brezhnev and Honecker kiss. It is free and iconic, but crowded, so go early. It is Wall history as much as art.
**The living scene is RAW-Gelände in Friedrichshain and Kreuzberg's big murals,** including Victor Ash's giant "Astronaut Cosmonaut" near Kottbusser Tor. Free, and best seen in daylight.
**For a rainy day there is Urban Nation,** a free street art museum on Bülowstraße in Schöneberg with murals on the street outside. Closed Mondays.

**The one real expedition is Teufelsberg,** an abandoned Cold War listening station in the Grunewald forest, often called Europe's largest open-air street art gallery. Entry is about €10 and it is skippable on a tight first visit.
